data.title: PDI: An Opportunity To Lock In A 14% Yield On Cost
data.description:
Income investors tend to focus on building out portfolios that generate recurring income and are often enticed by yields that are too good to be true. Sometimes, companies overextend themselves, and the dividend needs to be cut, or the macroeconomic changes cause the investment to no longer generate the same levels of distributable income. The PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (NYSE:PDI) has become a battleground on Seeking Alpha, as there are ratings throughout the entire spectrum. PDI is still one of my favorite income investments as it's never lowered the monthly distribution, no matter what has occurred in the macroeconomic cycle. PDI has distributed more income than its share price at the IPO and is still producing large amounts of monthly income. When it comes to closed-end funds (CEF) I look at what type of fund it is, how the assets are structured, and if it can keep churning out income with the prospects for appreciation. Income investments aren't for everyone, and PDI has given some investors buyer's remorse depending on when they made their initial investment, but shares look as if they have bottomed, and the strong distributable income is making this a potentially lucrative investment opportunity. I plan on adding to my position under $20 as I believe its book of assets will appreciate when the Fed starts cutting, and I plan on compounding the monthly distributions to generate additional income.

Following up on my previous article about PDI

I've been bullish on PDI for a while, and it's been a relatively good year. Since my previous article was published in February (can be read here), shares of PDI have declined by -6.01%, and after the distributions are factored in, its total return is -1.55% compared to the S&P 500 declining by -3%. I had discussed that PDI was offering investors a 13% yield from a distribution that had never been cut, and the net asset value (NAV) was starting to recover as the Fed reduced interest rates. 2025 hasn't been an easy investing environment, and I am following up with a new article to discuss why I am still bullish on PDI and using it to generate large amounts of income from my invested capital. I think the economy is setting up well for PDI and that shares will appreciate in the 2nd half of 2025 while maintaining their current distribution.

Risks to investing in PDI

While I am bullish on PDI, investors should do their own due diligence because there are several risk factors to consider. Something that doesn't get talked about enough is that PDI holds 1,900 investments, so doing the research on its entire portfolio is next to impossible. Investors don't necessarily know what they are buying, and some have never looked at even the top-10 holdings which represent 32.56% of the portfolio. PDI allocates capital across multiple fixed-income sectors globally, which include corporate debt obligations, mortgage and asset-backed securities, and government debt. Shifts in the macroeconomic environment and rising interest rates have devalued its NAV to a degree, as older debt trades at a discount to its face value. This has caused PDI's share value to erode over time, and just because the Fed looks like it will start cutting again sooner rather than later, they could keep rates where they are, which would negatively impact PDI. Investors should do their own research before allocating capital toward PDI because this isn't a typical investment where you need to research how 1 company operates. PDI owns a large book of assets, and you're putting your trust in their management team to navigate economic uncertainties.

Why I believe PDI is a solid income producing investment that will continue generating recurring income

I don't know of many income-producing assets that never decreased their distribution, maintained their current levels of income during the pandemic, and provided a yield that exceeded 10%. I didn't make my initial investment into PDI when shares were trading around $30, and my cost basis is under $20. I have been buying PDI for the CEF, it is today, not where it was previously. Today, PDI is generating a distribution of $2.65 per share, which is paid monthly to its investors. Based on the current share price, PDI's yield is 14.04%, and I am able to exceed 1% per month from its distribution. It's hard to complain about this when shares of PDI are up 2.59% on a YTD basis and reached $20.90 before the tariff talks started. I am confident that PDI will continue to generate recurring monthly income and see its share price increase over time.

When I look back at what PDI has been able to accomplish, it's very bullish for income investors. Yes, shares of PDI are down -24.60% since its IPO, but this is an income-focused investment, not one that revolves around capital appreciation. PDI went IPO for $25 back in May of 2012, and since then it's produced $40.96 of distributed income. Long-term investors who have owned PDI since its inception have generated 163.83% of their initial investment in distributed income and still own an asset base that is yielding over 14%. This is a winning combination, and that is exactly why sometimes investors need to look past the share price chart and go under the hood with the distributed income. I am expecting PDI to continue paying the large distribution, and I believe locking in a 14% yield on cost is a golden opportunity heading into the summer months.

Why I am bullish on PDI going forward

The recent price decline in shares of PDI is due to the market volatility caused by tariff and trade uncertainty. CEFs that focus on fixed income have been impacted because investor confidence has significantly decreased. Despite the drop in share prices, the current economic environment appears highly favorable for PDI's investment strategy. PDI is designed to thrive in uncertain conditions, leveraging PIMCO's expertise in active management and a diversified, high-quality fixed-income portfolio. PDI's focus on high-quality liquid assets such as U.S. agency mortgages which provides resilience against economic downturns and market volatility. Capital in PDI's portfolio has been weighted toward agency mortgages because they provide an advantage in yield over investment-grade corporate bonds and are backed by U.S. government agencies. This provides PDI's portfolio with liquidity and stability. PDI has also reduced its exposure to corporate credit due to tightening spreads and is focusing on strong fundamentals in investment-grade and high-yield sectors.

PDI's dynamic asset allocation strategy allows it to capitalize on mispriced securities and sector rotations, which can exploit market inefficiencies. The fund managers for PDI are allocating capital toward higher-quality emerging markets such as Mexico and South Africa. This allows PDI to maintain lower levels of currency risk while producing attractive yields. This provides flexibility that is valuable due to uncertainties around tariffs, fiscal policies, and global growth. The current economic environment with falling interest rates and high bond yields sets up well for PDI's strategy. Currently, its portfolio is being undervalued because there is still debt trading at a discount, and new debt being issued has a larger coupon than previously issued debt. As the Fed continues to lower rates, previously issued debt trading at a discount will become more enticing and will start to trade closer to face value, which should cause PDI's NAV and share price to increase. I think that PDI will get back to the $20s during 2025 and could continue higher as the macroeconomic environment improves.

Conclusion

I believe that shares of PDI are providing income investors with a golden opportunity as its book of assets could be significantly undervalued compared to what they could be worth as the Fed lowers rates. PDI has never reduced its distribution and is currently exceeding a 14% yield. Few investments are throwing off a distribution that provides investors with more than 1% per month in generated income. I believe the tariff and trade issues will be resolved, and the fixed income markets will settle down. As an income investor, I am more concerned about PDI's ability to generate recurring income, as long-term holders have benefited from having their asset base generate 163.83% of their initial investment in income while generating an annualized yield that exceeds 14%. The chart may be ugly, but that can turn around in the future, and I think PDI has a yield that isn't too good to be true.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 220: $22,000 Allocated, $2,213.76 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
This was a big week for the markets as there was a partial trade deal between the U.S. and China, which included temporary reductions in tariffs and commitments to agricultural purchases, providing a much-needed boost to global markets. President Trump secured several significant trade and investment deals in the Middle East, focusing on Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and the United Arab Emirates (UAE), which included a $600 billion investment from Saudi Arabia and a $1.2 trillion framework with Qatar for future trade and investment. This sent the S&P 500 up 2.63% as the Nasdaq climbed 3.6% this week. The price of oil is still under $65, and after listening to Fed Chair Powell's latest commentary, I am starting to get worried that the Fed is going to be late to the party once again. I believe they should have already cut rates as unemployment is climbing and inflation is getting closer to their 2% level. Moody's ended up downgrading the U.S. government's long-term issuer and senior unsecured ratings to Aa1 from Aaa, which scared the markets in the after-hours on Friday. I think that Moody's downgrading the debt is lacking in substance, and I am not worried about it. The bottom line is that if the U.S. defaults on debt, then we will have much bigger problems than fluctuations in the stock market. I believe that any pullback will be a buying opportunity and I am looking forward to adding to my positions.

The market had a strong week, and it was reflected throughout the portfolio, unlike last week. After allocating $22,00 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished in week 220 at $27,641.59. This was a weekly increase of 2.874% ($653.43), taking the account's profitability to $5,641.59, which is a return on invested capital of 25.64%. This week, I added Target (TGT) to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio after it was recommended during 2024. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $46.49 of dividend income in week 220. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $7.24 (0.33%) to $2,213.76. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $4,040.08 of income since inception, which is 18.36% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. The dividend snowball is in full effect as the weekly and monthly dividend income continues to increase on a YoY basis and I am excited to see how much forward dividend income is being generated at the end of June when the first half of 2025 is over.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

This was a great week for the markets and the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio as the account profitability increased 2.87% ($653.43) to $5,641.59. Despite the fluctuations, things are going well. A new all-time high portfolio balance of $27,641.59 was just reached, and the portfolio is generating $2,213.76 in forward dividend income. In the first couple of weeks in this series, there were many comments about how bad an idea this was or that you wouldn't be able to amass meaningful income from this strategy. I intentionally started this account with $100 and have added $100 each week to document how anyone could build an income-producing portfolio from scratch. My goals have been to generate recurring income while mitigating downside risk. I can't allocate more than 5% to a specific position or 20% to a single sector. I believe that the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is delivering on my goals, and it now has a portfolio yield of 8.01% and a yield on cost of 10.06%. The graph below shows its progress since inception, and I believe the results are very positive.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $663.04 (29.95%)
  • Equities $548.59 (24.78%)
  • CEFs $391.63 (17.69%)
  • REITs $368.53 (16.65%)
  • BDCs $241.97 (10.93%)

Collecting dividends can serve many functions in a portfolio. Some investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off of them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 20 weeks in 2025, $861.70 of dividend income was produced, which is 53.09% of the total income from 2024. There were 280 positions that paid a dividend, which is 39.49% of the dividends generated in 2024.

20 weeks have passed since the start of the year, and the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $46.49 of dividend income this week. My weekly average for dividend income in 2025 is $43.09, which is an increase of 38.03% ($11.87) compared to the 2024 weekly average of $31.21 of dividend income being produced. The amount of income being produced in 2025 looks completely different than in 2024, and the dividend snowball effect is showcasing how continuously investing and reinvesting the dividends can drive larger dividends in the future. I think it's entirely possible that the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio exceeds $2,500 in forward dividend income by the end of 2025, and $125 is exceeded as the new high on weekly income.

What's interesting is that only 2 weeks have past, and I have already hit $100.83 of dividend income being generated in May. Last year, I generated $119.92 of dividend income, and it's going to be interesting to see where things settle this time around. There are still 2 weeks left in May, and I have already generated $827.68 in dividend income, which is 41.68% ($243.50) more than the first 5 months of 2024. The progression is surreal and I think that I will be able to exceed several milestones in the 2nd half of 2025. I am looking forward to reassessing some of my previous projections once June is over and I have all of the data from the first half of the year.

This week there were 44 positions generating at least 1 share from their dividends on an annual basis within the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. The new shares are expected to produce $227.18 in forward dividend income within the portfolio. I have been working on several positions, and I am hoping to have at least 50 positions in the green section of the table below by the end of the year. I still believe there is a lot of value to be unlocked within the portfolio, and I want to get Kinder Morgan (KMI) back into the green section and have BP p.l.c. (BP) cross over before the summer begins.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs and REITs remain neck and neck, and I don't see this changing any time soon. Individual equities now represent 38.78% of the Dividend Harvesting portfolio while generating 24.78% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 61.22% of the portfolio and generate 75.22% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5–10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding to energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

Altria Group (MO) took a bit of a dive this week on how much of the portfolio it represents as it went from 4.83% to 4.64%. I really would like the top-end of the portfolio to even out a bit further. From position 5-20, I think the fluctuations in how much each position represents are great, and I would like a more even flow into the top-4 positions over time. I am not sure how long it's going to take. I think that the top-20 positions are going to change a bit over the next several months, as I see a bunch of opportunities I am looking to allocate capital toward. The long-term goal is to get all the positions under 4%, even though I have allowed myself to go as high as 5%.

The PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI) has exited the top-10 positions, and SL Green Realty (SLG) has made it back on the list. There were several positions that paid dividends this week, which made the table below change a bit. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,225.22, and they finished the week with a value of $8,307.91. This is an ROI of $2,082.69 (33.46%). There has been $1,030 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 16.55% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $615.22 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 9.88%. These positions represent 30.06% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 27.79% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 220 Additions

In week 220 I added Target (TGT) to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio.

Target

  • Last week, I wrote a dedicated article about TGT (can be read here) about why I was very bullish on its forward prospects
  • TGT has declined by more than -60% since its peak in 2021 and its trading is under 11 times 2025 earnings, with a dividend yield that exceeds 4.5%
  • TGT's growth plan includes $15 billion in sales growth by 2030 through investments in private labels, digital experience, and store expansions
  • I believe that TGT will rebound, considering it has better margins than Walmart (WMT) and Costco (COST), and is optimizing its business for growth

Week 221 game plan

In week 221, I am planning on adding to the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) and FS KKR Capital Corp (FSK).

Conclusion

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has reached a new all-time high as its account value has surpassed $27,600 while generating $2,213.76 in forward dividend income. When I look at the charts below, the dividend snowball continues to increase, and an S-curve in dividend income is forming. I'm excited for the future of this account as the monthly projected income is approaching $200. I think that the portfolio is positioned well for the future and has shown that it can mitigate downside risk while generating recurring income. There are a bunch of positions I am looking to add to prior to the Fed starting its next cutting cycle, as I believe this will be a big catalyst for many positions I am holding. I am planning on updating my initial predictions for 2025 once June is over, as I believe I may have been too conservative on some items. Please leave all of your comments below as I try to interact with everyone in the comments section.

The Net Expenses is the actual fund expense ratio applicable to investors. Not all classes of shares may be available to all investors or through all broker-dealer platforms.

Market Review

The initial wave of optimism over the incoming U.S. administration’s deregulation and pro-growth policies dissipated over the quarter, as the sequence of tariff announcements, retractions, and delays created uncertainty over trade policies. This uncertainty was echoed in data that indicated a slowdown in spending, cautious outlooks from companies, and lower economic projections from the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed). Uncertainty from tariff policies pressured U.S. equities, resulting in the S&P 500 Index’s 4.27% decline in a volatile quarter.

Market participants were initially enthused over the prospect of Trump-era deregulation and pro-growth policies, leading to equity market gains early in the quarter. Post-inauguration, the Trump administration has been focused on reshaping trade relationships to encourage increased domestic manufacturing and lowered trade deficits. To that end, 25% tariffs were announced on imports from Mexico and Canada, 20% tariffs on goods from China, and 25% tariffs on foreign-made automobiles. In addition, future reciprocal tariffs on the aforementioned countries, along with the European Union, were telegraphed. With tariffs levied on America’s largest trade partners, investors feared the likely passing of higher costs to consumers could dampen economic growth and prompt an uptick in inflation. The wide-ranging impact of tariffs on the automotive, retail, and home construction industries, amongst others, increased uncertainty and risk. As investors began to adjust to the possibility of a prolonged trade war, expectations of slower growth pressured equities.

The Fed held the Federal Funds Rate constant at 4.25-4.50% during its January and March Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meetings, with members acknowledging that U.S. disinflation has slowed. The pause in its monetary easing campaign allows the Fed more time to evaluate the impact of Trump’s policies on the economy and inflation. The released FOMC economic projections reflect the Fed’s expectations that these policies will have negative economic impacts, including 2025 gross domestic product growth lowering from 2.1% to 1.7%, inflation rising from 2.5% to 2.7%, and projected unemployment increasing from 4.3% to 4.4%.

The fourth quarter earnings season saw the topic of tariffs rise to the fore, while reiterating the themes of a weakening consumer and continued artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure build-out. Broadly, corporations reported revenue and profit growth, but management teams warned of a slowing consumer amid uncertainty and high interest rates. On the topic of AI, the largest technology companies announced plans to invest an even greater amount into AI in the year ahead to meet burgeoning demand. Companies cited success in using AI in automation and aiding workers, two sources of productivity enhancement. These results and positive outlook helped alleviate concerns related to January’s DeepSeek-driven equity sell-off over reduced AI computing demand as cheaper Chinese alternatives are developed. Tariffs were also widely discussed during the earnings season, as some companies expect to pass on cost increases to consumers while others plan to absorb the additional costs. The uncertainty around tariffs also led many companies to delay their capital expenditure plans until there is more clarity and issue more muted outlooks. The uncertainty related to global trade policies weighed on investor sentiment and led to equity markets selling off in the quarter.

Performance Review

The Fund outperformed the Index in the period, driven by an overall positive stock selection effect. Within financials, an overweight allocation paired with strong performance from the Fund’s holdings within the sector resulted in positive allocation and selection effects, which boosted relative performance. The Fund’s holdings across the industrials sector were additive to performance. In the consumer discretionary sector, a positive stock selection effect offset a negative allocation effect and boosted relative results.

Conversely, negative stock selection effects within the health care and communication services sectors overshadowed positive allocation effects and ultimately detracted from relative results. The Fund’s underweight of the consumer staples sector led to a negative allocation effect.

Top Contributors

The top contributors to relative performance include BAE Systems, Progressive (PGR), Chevron, Visa, and Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A).

Top Detractors

The top detractors from relative performance include Apple, NVIDIA, Alphabet, Amazon.com, and Microsoft.

Market Outlook

We expect continued uncertainty, and, consequently, volatility, in the equity markets due to the dynamic nature of tariff policy. The U.S. is seeking to reshape its relationship with trade partners and allies through changes in trade policies, foreign relations, domestic fiscal spending, and monetary policies. The ultimate cadence and degree of this transformation are unknown. The near-term market backdrop remains uncertain and is contributing to a volatile market environment. From our decades of investment experience, we continue to believe that markets will likely reward companies with strong fundamentals and adept management teams that can navigate these policy uncertainties. We are confident that the businesses we invest in are well positioned to adjust to and manage these economic conditions.

The Fund’s investment philosophy remains disciplined during periods of heightened risk and volatility. We believe our steady investment approach may help investors to participate meaningfully in periods of market expansion while also potentially benefiting from defensive characteristics in periods of economic and political uncertainty. We find solace in our ability to identify and invest in what we consider to be companies with strong fundamental characteristics: strong balance sheets, consistent earnings growth, market leadership, and headed by management teams that we believe can navigate through periods of change and adapt accordingly.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a mutual fund carefully before investing. Investing in foreign denominated and/or domiciled securities involves special risks, including changes in currency exchange rates, political, economic, and social instability, limited company information, differing auditing and legal standards, and less market liquidity. These risks generally are greater with emerging market countries.
